source.title: Remote UX Research Guide [version for Ksyusha, Seryozha, and Arseniy]

blocks.text: This guide will help you earn business support and integrate research in a way that speeds work up instead of slowing it down. You will learn best practices from strong research teams, run your first fast and accessible remote UX study, and get feedback from people whose opinions truly matter.

We will focus on remote research: respondents complete tasks and answer questions on their own device, while you immediately receive the data: answers, completion time, clicks, heatmaps, and much more.
This lets you research almost anything:
copy, features, and interfaces
ideas and concepts, including competitor solutions
users’ motivations and barriers, so you can choose the right direction
If research feels complicated, do not worry. We will break the process into steps and make it simple and clear.
Why research matters and how to convince the business of its value
Simple answers to common stakeholder objections
Everything about remote research
How to prepare an unmoderated study: from defining the business problem and hypotheses to writing respondent tasks
How to work with respondents: where to find them, how many you need, and how to motivate them
Which prototypes are suitable for tests and a checklist for preparing them
How to analyze results, write a report, and bring insights into the product
How AI already helps researchers
A step-by-step plan for integrating research: from the first quick tests to a systematic practice
UX maturity models and ways to assess the team’s current level
Small and mid-sized teams: which research development strategy to choose, with many examples, including how to handle stakeholder objections, plus a chance to run your first test.
Teams with an already mature practice: new ideas and techniques for optimizing processes and strengthening research culture.
Teams implementing democratization: what has worked in practice.
To spend less on mistakes.
Every hour “saved” on research will come back as bugs, rework, user churn, or even product failure.
In a startup, a strategic mistake can burn through the entire budget before product–market fit.
In a corporation, it can cost market share and shareholder value.
In both cases, delaying the release of the right solution is a direct business risk.

Research is often called a “luxury” that is easy to cut for the sake of speed. Deadlines are tight, stakeholders ask, “Can we just launch?” But launching without validation is a blind bet, where it is easier to miss than to hit the target.
When a team has data, not just opinions, decisions are made faster. Discussions turn into conversations about facts, and everyone — from product to marketing — moves in the same direction.

Skipping research is not saving money; it is moving validation to users after release. Only then they “test” the product not with insights, but with churn and negative reviews. Of course, not everything needs to be researched, but the key things do.

chapters.title: Resistance to research and how to work with it

blocks.text: If a team resists research, that is a normal working situation. Many researchers in professional communities say they face it regularly. Below, we have collected the most common objections and ways to respond to them.

“Research is too complicated.” Where do we start? Which method should we choose? Where do we find the right users? What if they turn out to be irrelevant? How do we analyze everything afterward? At the beginning, there may be more questions than answers. But research does not have to be heavy or cumbersome. In this guide, we will break the process down step by step and walk you through launching your first study.

“It is expensive.” Some research is no longer a luxury: some capabilities are free, and even paid tests pay for themselves by saving money on fixing mistakes. Finding a problem before release can be dozens of times more cost-effective than fixing it in a finished product.

On Pathway, the first 3 projects are free. If you run a study with your own audience, up to 250 responses per month are also free. All features are available: flexible logic, split tests, filters, and analysis tools.
On average, a test on Pathway costs [add figure].
Market example: Sun Microsystems spent about $20,000 improving usability and saved $152 million. This means every dollar invested returned $7,500.

“We do not have time or capacity.” Product managers always have many parallel priorities. That is why research should fit naturally into their workflow: short formats, clear takeaways, and minimal extra load. Even one quick test saves more development hours than it takes to run.

“We already take too long to get started.” It may feel like research only slows the work down: while we prepare a scenario and audience, the solution is already due for release. Today, in a few hours, you can test a prototype, receive audio and video recordings of sessions, or launch a survey and immediately see the first responses.
Market example: IBM showed in a report that finding a defect before release is 30 times more cost-effective than fixing it in an already released product.
“Not everything needs to be tested.” That is true: you do not need to check every button or chart color.
It is important to focus on risky decisions:
things that are fundamentally new and have not yet been validated in the product,
functionality without direct analogues,
changes that can strongly affect key metrics or users’ perception of the product.

These are exactly the things worth testing first. This reduces the fear that “we are losing speed” and, on the contrary, helps the team move faster: small fixes can be shipped immediately, while new or debatable decisions are better validated in advance so you do not lose more time and resources later.
“Five people is not serious. We have millions of users; we need at least thousands, otherwise the tests will not be statistically significant.”

This objection most often appears when people talk about usability tests. Here it is important to remember: their goal is to find the main interaction problems, not to calculate percentages. You do not need thousands of users for that. Even 5–7 respondents help reveal most critical issues.
When is that not enough?
Unmoderated tests. In remote research, some recordings may be low quality, so it is worth increasing the sample size.
Different segments. If you need to compare groups, for example new and experienced users, each group should have its own minimum number of respondents.

Statistical validation. When the goal is numbers with confidence intervals, you will need many more participants: dozens, hundreds, or even thousands. But that is a different type of research: quantitative research.
We talk more about the number of respondents for different types of tests here →

“This is not our audience, just some unclear respondents.” Pathway monitors research participants and their answers every day and filters out bad-faith respondents using heuristics, trap questions, and manual review. More about quality control here →
To make sure respondents fit your task, set up filters and add screening questions. They will help you find the right people. More about finding the right audience →
“It is hard to find respondents.” Today, finding respondents is easier than ever. In Pathway, you can:
Share a test link with your own audience. Unlimited responses are free. Just copy the research link in your account and share it with your audience in any convenient way.
Connect your own verified external panel.
Use the Pathway panel — a couple of clicks and it is ready.
Need a rare audience? We are here to help, and you can always ask us to find any audience you need. Just tell us who you need to attract, and we will handle the rest. Details here →

“Why? We know what to do, and the stakeholders approved the release.” Even if the team is confident in the solution and stakeholders have given the green light, unmoderated research is a fast and inexpensive insurance policy against serious mistakes. Surveys help check whether hypotheses really match users’ perception. Remote usability tests show how the product works in reality — outside the team’s assumptions.

Market example: Fixing a problem at the design stage is 10 times cheaper than at the development stage and 100 times cheaper than after release (Gilb, 1988). In one case, skipping early testing cost the company $165M: the project had to be closed because a critical flaw was discovered too late. More examples →
“We already know everything about our users.” We are not our users. Even when we feel we understand them, in practice:
A button that is “right there” can go unnoticed.
A simple scenario can suddenly turn into a dead end.
A small thing that seems trivial to us can become a reason to leave.
A product you personally cannot live without may be needed only by a narrow segment.
“We do not have dedicated researchers.” With this guide and Pathway, you will launch your first study.

Resistance to research decreases when the team has facts, examples, and its own positive experience. But it takes time: to get used to new processes, overcome doubts, and move past old beliefs. The best way to handle it is to stay in dialogue, acknowledge feelings, and calmly keep doing what matters for the product. And the earlier you start researching, the more time, money, and energy you will save for yourself and the team.

All the research we discuss below takes place online. It can be moderated or unmoderated, and also qualitative or quantitative. Each format has its own purpose and its own place in the life of a product.

In moderated research, the researcher communicates with the respondent directly — through video calls or messengers. These are always qualitative methods that help you understand users’ motivations, context, and barriers more deeply.
The main formats include:
Moderated usability tests — check how users complete tasks in a prototype or product.
In-depth interviews — explore experience and perception in detail.
Focus groups — let you discuss a product or idea with a small group.
Diary studies — capture people’s experience over several days or weeks.
Observations — show real behavior in a natural environment.

In moderated usability tests, the researcher meets with each user and runs the session using a guide. This format works when you need not only to measure scenario completion, but also to understand motivation, expectations, and barriers.
The advantage of moderated tests is that they let you work even with “rough” or imperfect prototypes.
There are two approaches:

Structured testing — you have clear tasks and a fixed order for completing them. This format is useful for comparing participants and checking all hypotheses, but it requires knowing the user tasks in advance and leaves little room for unexpected insights.

Unstructured testing — the guide is approximate, and the researcher follows the respondent’s actions and tasks. This gives flexibility and can uncover unexpected insights, but the results are harder to compare with each other and there is a risk of moving away from the main research goal.
Moderated research requires more time and resources than unmoderated research, but it provides a depth that is impossible to get without a live dialogue.
Here, respondents complete tasks on their own: they go through a scenario, answer questions, and compare interfaces.
This format can be both qualitative and quantitative — it all depends on the goal.
A moderator is not needed during the study. They only need to prepare the study at the start — formulate the tasks correctly so the team gets the data it needs.
Here, the respondent also completes tasks on their own, but AI takes on the role of moderator.
For example, Pathway AI can ask follow-up questions, run a mini-interview, and collect detailed answers. As a result, the researcher spends less time, and the insights become deeper.

children.title: When unmoderated research is a good fit

blocks.text: Check whether the interface is clear: buttons, icons, and copy.
Compare several options, such as banners, screens, or messages, by attractiveness, clarity, or other metrics and, for example, select variants for an A/B test.
Understand how easy it is to find an element: account login, the “Buy” button.
Test a simple linear scenario: registration or checkout in e-commerce.
Check a prototype before release: find problems and optimize steps.
Validate usability-test findings quantitatively, for example by surveying more people to confirm a hypothesis about why a button label is confusing.
Validate a hypothesis quantitatively: survey more people after a usability test.
Run a “check” of a website or app: find bottlenecks and conversion barriers.
Check finished copy: marketing copy, value proposition, email.
A good fit when:
you need to collect many responses quickly,
you need a diverse audience on a limited budget,
you need to observe behavior in a natural environment without moderator influence.

children.title: When it is better to choose a moderated format

blocks.text: To understand needs, motives, and barriers, and to explore previous experience. In a survey you can ask about experience, or before an unmoderated test you can ask people to describe it, but without a live dialogue the result will be superficial.

To understand why an interface error occurs. An unmoderated test will show the result, but will not deeply explain the cause. An interview or moderated usability test lets you hear expectations and the context behind actions.

Long and complex scenarios are easier to test in a moderated format, especially when there are different branches and you need to understand the reasons behind actions. But if the scenario is linear or split into parts, respondents will not get confused, or the goal is only to measure completion, an unmoderated format can work too.
To work with an audience that is not used to online tools. If the product audience is not very “digital,” an unmoderated format can scare people away and make the results unnatural.

To discuss sensitive topics. In personal or emotionally difficult questions, people are more comfortable talking to a moderator. For example, in mental-health apps, users may share personal experiences, and without live contact with the researcher it may be harder for them to feel supported and safe.
In general, unmoderated research is not suitable for every case where you need to talk to a user; for that, you need moderated research.

Choose the correct statement about unmoderated research. A. It can be used only for qualitative methods. B. It always requires a moderator to be present. C. It can be both qualitative and quantitative. D. It can be run only on finished products. Show the correct answer.

Choose the incorrect statement. A. Unmoderated research is convenient on a limited budget. B. Moderated research is better for discussing sensitive topics. C. In long, branching scenarios, it is more convenient to use an unmoderated format. D. AI-moderated research can ask follow-up questions automatically. Show the correct answer.

Match the task with the suitable method. Check whether users understand how to complete registration. Find out why people stop filling their cart. Discuss a new tariff with active customers. Options: A. Unmoderated test. B. Moderated usability test. C. Focus group. Show the correct answer.
For research to be useful, you need a plan — a research design. It shows in advance which answers you will get and how they will help you make a decision.
the business problem
the main research goal
research questions and hypotheses
methods
audience: how to choose it and calculate the right number of participants
prototypes: what types exist and how to prepare them for testing
timeline
how the results will be used
who can be responsible for what: product managers, designers, editors, analysts, and marketers
This document connects all parts of the study and makes it meaningful: you spend time not for the sake of “process,” but for clear numbers, insights, and business value.
To start filling out your brief, download the template →
The first step in any study is to understand the business problem. Why is the study needed? What problem does it solve? Which product priorities is it connected to?
Focusing on the business problem helps you:
set priorities correctly
choose the right research method
focus only on what truly moves the team forward
Talk to the stakeholder, or ask yourself, and try to formulate the business problem as best you can. The main thing is to keep the business interests in focus, even if the wording is not perfect yet.
Example questions for clarifying the business problem
Example problems: Increase conversion from free users to paid users. Reduce the percentage of abandoned orders. Reduce support load. Increase registration conversion.
Dasha Lupakhina, Head of Product Research at Profi.ru, explained in the Pathway blog how to make research a lever for business decisions →
When the business problem is clear, move to the next step: define the main research goal.
Example questions for clarifying the main research goal
As an example, let’s take business problems and add the main research goal to each of them.

Business problem: increase conversion from free users to paid users. Main research goal: understand how users perceive the interface and the scenarios for moving to the paid version, and identify barriers that prevent them from completing payment.
Business problem: reduce the percentage of abandoned orders. Main research goal: understand which usability factors prevent users from completing the process.
Business problem: reduce support load. Main research goal: understand how easy it is to find the needed information in the FAQ.

Business problem: increase registration conversion. Main research goal: understand whether the authorization process is fast and error-free for users, and identify barriers that make them abandon it halfway.

Pro tip: Before moving on, discuss with the team what actions they see for different research outcomes. Sometimes it turns out that colleagues expect confirmation of their assumptions and are not ready for another result. Discussing this in advance helps you understand whether there is a plan B, decide whether the research is really needed, and prepare for the next steps.
If you are testing a prototype or a real website, start with product-use scenarios — larger user actions that people perform to solve their task.
This will help you:
identify the key tasks that are most important to test first
not forget secondary scenarios that also affect the experience
formulate hypotheses more precisely — they will be based on real user actions
You can document a scenario in different ways:
as plain text,
as a flowchart,
or as a chain of Figma screens you want to test.

The main thing is to understand and document the scenarios in advance. Make sure they are connected to the main research goal and the business problem, and that testing them will actually help solve it.

Scenario example: Your product is a tutor-search service, and the target audience is parents of schoolchildren who have little time and usually search in the evening after work. Business problem: increase conversion from search to booking the first lesson. Main research goal: identify the scenario steps that prevent parents from quickly finding and choosing a tutor, and understand how to remove them. Parent scenarios: Filter math tutors on the platform. Share options with the child. Return to saved profiles. Book the first lesson.
A hypothesis is a reasoned assumption that needs to be tested. It is formulated as a statement about people, a product, or an interface.

Hypothesis examples: Users will understand that to apply for a tax deduction, they need to click the “Deductions” button on the main screen and then “Apply for tax deduction.” Users will understand that the banner on the home page invites them to subscribe for discounts. New users most often come through friends’ recommendations, not through ads. Users abandon the cart at the delivery-selection step because they do not see a convenient option.
A research question is needed when there is no hypothesis and you want to find a new solution or better understand behavior.
Research question examples: Which delivery method do most people prefer? How will users understand the button label? Which offer variant looks more trustworthy?

It is better to formulate a hypothesis or research question when you are just beginning to think about the study. This helps you understand whether you really need user research rather than, for example, an A/B test; which research method can test the hypothesis or answer the research question; how to formulate the test task; and how to structure and analyze the results after the test.

children.title: How to understand whether a hypothesis or question is formulated correctly

blocks.text: Check your version against these five rules:
Behavior is more precise than emotions
New knowledge
Formulated specifically
One hypothesis — one statement
The results will be used
If at least one rule is not met, it is better to reformulate the hypothesis or question.
Behavior is more precise than emotions. Ratings such as “liked it” do not provide reliable data — look at what people do and how they do it.

Hypothesis: The user likes button A more than button B. Users clicked button A faster than button B. Question: Which button does the user like more? Which button do users click faster and more often: A or B?
New knowledge. If the answer is already known from analytics or experience, testing it is pointless. A good hypothesis clarifies something the team does not yet understand.

Hypothesis: Users understand that the magnifying-glass icon means search. Users understood that the text “Let’s go” in search means submitting a request. Question: Do people like doing sports? What prevents users from starting to exercise?
Formulated specifically. You need to clarify exactly what you are testing.

Users will understand the text on the button. Users understood that delivery was postponed by one day after seeing the text on the button. Or: The design of the authorization page is fine. Users found the “Log in” button without hints. Users completed authorization on the first attempt.
One hypothesis — one statement. There should not be two ideas inside one hypothesis at the same time.
Users understood the new name and consider it more convenient than the old one. Users understood what the new name means. And: Users consider the new name more convenient than the old one.
The results will be used. If nothing will change even after you learn the answer, the study is not worth launching.
Find out which icon is clearer if developers will not change it. Find out which icon is clearer if improvements are possible.
If the hypothesis or research question passes all five checks, it is ready to use.

chapters.title: Methods: how to choose and launch

blocks.text: At this stage, you already have a list of research questions and hypotheses. If not, read the chapter “Preparing for research.”
To choose the right method, match each question with one or more methods. This will give you a step-by-step plan and reduce the risk of missing something.
Below is a short overview of popular unmoderated research methods, including methods you can launch through Pathway.
Do not limit yourself to the pattern “one question = one method.” Often, to get the full picture, it is worth checking one question in several ways.
Two columns: Research question or hypothesis — Method ALT

rows: Unmoderated Scenario-Based Test
the respondent completes a task in a prototype or on a website independently, without a moderator. For example: “Set up the order so it arrives by dinner.”
It shows whether users understand the interface, easily find the needed elements, complete the scenario without errors, and perceive the path as convenient.
Preference test
the respondent is shown several variants — screens, copy, or images — and asked to choose the one that seems better. For example: “Which option looks more beneficial?”
It helps understand the perception of different solutions, but not usability: which option inspires more trust and looks more attractive, which copy or banners attract more attention.
A/B usability test

respondents are shown different versions of the same screen or scenario — for example, variant A and variant B. Each group completes the same task, and you compare the results: who completed it faster, made fewer mistakes, and where users felt more confident.
The method helps determine which interface variant works better — not by taste, but by actual use.
Card sorting
the respondent is shown cards with product elements and asked to group them by meaning in the way they would expect to see them on a website or in an app.
It helps design navigation from scratch or check whether the current navigation matches users’ logic.
First Click
the respondent sees an interface screen and completes a task: find and click the element they would start with.
It helps understand whether the design is structured correctly and whether users understand where to click to solve the task.
Five-Second Test
the respondent is shown a screen or page for 5 seconds, sometimes longer if the content is complex, and then answers questions.

It helps evaluate the first impression of a page, screen, or product: what people notice immediately, whether they understand the meaning, and how clearly the brand or product message is communicated.

blocks.text: Method. How it works. Helps validate. Unmoderated Scenario-Based Test: the respondent completes a task in a prototype or on a website independently, without a moderator. For example: “Set up the order so it arrives by dinner.” It shows whether users understand the interface, easily find the needed elements, complete the scenario without errors, and perceive the path as convenient. Preference test: the respondent is shown several variants — screens, copy, or images — and asked to choose the one that seems better. For example: “Which option looks more beneficial?” It helps understand the perception of different solutions, but not usability: which option inspires more trust and looks more attractive, which copy or banners attract more attention. A/B usability test: respondents are shown different versions of the same screen or scenario — for example, variant A and variant B. Each group completes the same task, and you compare the results: who completed it faster, made fewer mistakes, and where users felt more confident. The method helps determine which interface variant works better — not by taste, but by actual use. Card sorting: the respondent is shown cards with product elements and asked to group them by meaning in the way they would expect to see them on a website or in an app. It helps design navigation from scratch or check whether the current navigation matches users’ logic. First Click: the respondent sees an interface screen and completes a task: find and click the element they would start with. It helps understand whether the design is structured correctly and whether users understand where to click to solve the task. Five-Second Test: the respondent is shown a screen or page for 5 seconds, sometimes longer if the content is complex, and then answers questions. It helps evaluate the first impression of a page, screen, or product: what people notice immediately, whether they understand the meaning, and how clearly the brand or product message is communicated.
The respondent completes a task in a prototype or on a website independently, without a moderator.

Scenario testing helps check: ✔︎ Whether users understand the interface and find the needed elements without hints. ✔︎ Whether they can complete the scenario on their own, in how many steps, and how much time it takes. ✔︎ Where errors, difficulties, or incorrect actions occur. ✔︎ Whether the path feels simple and convenient, and where users most often drop off.

For example, you redesigned a cart screen and want to check whether users understand how to choose the delivery method and time, and then confirm at checkout that everything is set correctly. Respondent task: “Imagine you ordered dinner to your home and want it to arrive tomorrow evening when you are at home. Set up the order so that this happens.” Follow-up questions after completion: • How easy was it to find the needed settings? (method: scale) • What seemed unclear or unnecessary? (method: question)
Does not help check:
It does not always answer “why” — the reasons for errors are only partly visible through the path or final comments.
What to consider when running it:
The test takes 5–10 minutes; the optimal minimum is 15 respondents.
The more complex the scenario and the more possible paths it has, the more sense it makes to run a moderated study.
It is better to split the prototype into short tasks so the person does not get confused.
Add a separate “success screen” to capture the result.
The device used for the test should match the prototype.
Consider privacy when setting up the test: not everyone is comfortable turning on a camera, and this can also affect completion conversion.
The method does not always answer “why” — the reasons for errors are only partly visible through the path or final comments.
We explain prototypes in more detail in the chapter “Prototypes: what types exist and how to prepare them for testing.”

In Pathway, you can test Figma prototypes or live websites. In the Help Center, we explain how to set up a Live website block and what you will get in the report. More about Live website: how to set it up and what you will get in the report.
The respondent is shown several variants — screens, copy, or images — and asked to choose the one that seems better. For example: “Which option looks more beneficial?”

The method helps understand the perception of different solutions. It is not about usability, but about impressions: trust, emotional tone, attractiveness. For competitive products, these nuances become part of the strategy because they directly influence the sense of convenience and trust in the brand.
✔︎ Which option inspires more trust and looks more attractive. ✔︎ Which copy or banners attract more attention. ✔︎ Which icons or illustrations feel more appropriate for the product.

For example, you are testing the design of a promo banner for a campaign. You have two variants: • One uses a photo with people. • The other uses a bright illustration. The respondent is shown both variants and asked: “Which of these two banner options makes you trust it more?”
What to consider when using the method:
Compare only one detail at a time. If the variants differ in too many ways, it will be impossible to understand what influenced the choice.
The change should be noticeable, not cosmetic. It is better not to test close color shades, fonts, or button labels — the difference may be too subtle.
Phrase the question so that it sets the comparison criterion — for example, “Which option feels more emotional?” or “Which one inspires more trust?”
When analyzing results, check statistical significance to make sure the difference between variants is not random. More details are in the chapter.
Do not compare your own screens with other services. Differences in logic and style are too large, and the results will be incorrect.
Remember that Preference tests and A/B tests provide different information. The first reflects subjective perception; the second reflects real behavior. The results may not match.
What results you will get:
The percentage of people who selected each screen.
Detailed respondent comments explaining why they chose one option or another.
How to identify the winning variant

To do this, estimate the margins of error for the shares of respondents who chose each option and present the values as intervals. You can do this with our calculator. If the intervals overlap, a statistically significant difference has not been proven. If the intervals do not overlap, the winner is the option selected by the largest number of respondents.

Respondents are shown different versions of the same screen or scenario — for example, variant A and variant B. Each group completes the same task, and you compare the results: who completed it faster, made fewer mistakes, and where users felt more confident.
The method helps determine which interface variant works better — not by taste, but by actual use.
For example:
✔︎ Which page version helps users complete the scenario faster. ✔︎ Which button layout reduces errors. ✔︎ Which option generates more conversions.
How it differs from a preference test:
In an A/B test, people perform a task and you measure effectiveness. In a preference test, users simply choose which option they like visually or which one seems clearer.
What to consider when using the method

For reliable results, it is better to recruit enough respondents in each group. We explain how to calculate the required number of respondents in the chapter “How many respondents you need for quantitative research.”

The key in an A/B test is to keep the comparison clean: one person should not participate in both versions. In Pathway, this is handled automatically through a split group that divides respondents into non-overlapping groups.
The respondent is shown cards with product elements and asked to group them by meaning in the way they would expect to see them on a website or in an app.
In closed sorting, the groups are predefined.
In open sorting, respondents create the groups and their names themselves.
The method helps not only design a structure from scratch, but also check how well the existing navigation matches the way users group information:
✔︎ How well do product navigation and structure match users’ logic?
✔︎ Which categories or sections should be merged or renamed?
✔︎ What is the best way to organize catalog and menu navigation?
✔︎ Which elements are difficult to place?

For example, you want to update a clothing catalog. Give respondents cards with wardrobe items and observe: in open sorting, how they group the cards themselves (“everyday clothing,” “office,” “accessories”); in closed sorting, how they distribute the same cards into predefined categories.
What to consider when using the method:
Sorting does not give you a ready final menu — you need to analyze the grouping logic, find patterns, and only then build the structure.
Number of cards: 30–50 is optimal. If there are too many cards, respondents get tired.
Run it with several people: one result will not tell you anything, but with 20–30 participants stable patterns will appear.
What results you will get:
For open sorting: catalog categories or menu items identified by respondents.
For closed sorting: the distribution of elements across groups made by respondents.

We explain card sorting in more detail in the Pathway Help Center → This link lets you take an open card-sorting test from the respondent’s perspective, and this one lets you view a report for a similar test. Here you can find a closed card-sorting test, take it, and then study the report for a similar test via this link.

The respondent sees an interface screen and completes a task: find and click the element they would start with. The method shows whether the design is structured correctly and whether users understand where to click to solve their task, for example: ✔︎ How easy it is to find an element on the page. ✔︎ How respondents use navigation, for example to understand which menu item people look in for the needed action. ✔︎ How effective the menu is, for example whether the respondent can find a specific product in it.
What to consider when using the method:
Do not use button names or menu item labels in questions. Where would you click to pay? Imagine you want to buy this product. What would you do?
Compare screens with statistical significance in mind. Without checking it, differences may be random. More details in the chapter “What to do with test results.”
Add comments to clicks. Ask “Why did you click exactly here?” — this helps understand motivation and hidden logic that is not visible on the click map.
Evaluate choice speed. It matters not only where people clicked, but also how quickly. Long hesitation may indicate doubts or an interface problem.
What results you will get:
The main metric: the percentage of users who made the correct first click.
Additionally, you can look at where people clicked incorrectly and which elements competed for attention.
Pro tip: Click time can be used only as additional context, for example to notice obvious hesitation or misunderstanding. But first of all, focus on success rate.
The respondent is shown a screen or page for 5 seconds, sometimes longer if the content is complex, and then answers questions. The method helps understand:

✔︎ What first impression the page creates. ✔︎ Which elements users notice immediately. ✔︎ How they understand the meaning and purpose of the page or product. ✔︎ How clearly the message is communicated and whether it matches brand expectations.
Example task before viewing an image in Pathway ALT
What to consider when using the method:
Do not hint at what exactly people should notice or remember.
Do not tell respondents in advance that the screen will be shown for only 5 seconds — simply limit the display time.

After the display, ask 1–3 open questions, for example: What do you remember from this page? What do you think this product, website, or screen is? What did you like or, on the contrary, find confusing?
If the interface contains a lot of text or many elements, increase the time to 10–20 seconds to keep a balance between first impression and understanding.
To compare different design versions, for example two landing pages, a Preference test is a better fit.
What results you will get:
Qualitative answers: what people remembered, how they described the page in their own words, and what emotions they felt.
Quantitative data: the share of participants who correctly identified the page goal, noticed key elements, or mentioned the brand.

Pro tip: You can analyze the median viewing duration of your specific landing page or target screen and adjust the timing to real user behavior. Sometimes 5 seconds is too little or, on the contrary, too much. Try testing several durations and see how the results change.

children.title: Other methods that help measure opinion, attitude, satisfaction, and experience

blocks.text: Above, we covered the main unmoderated research methods. In many of them, after scenarios or tasks, you can add multiple-choice questions, satisfaction scales, or open questions to understand what people felt and thought.

Before the study begins, you can also learn about the respondent’s experience to interpret the results correctly: whether the person has used the product, faced similar tasks, or belongs to the target audience.
The same question types can also be used in separate surveys when you are not testing an interface but want to collect users’ opinions or experience.

The respondent evaluates an object or experience on a scale — numeric, emoji-based, or star-based. The method helps measure subjective perception: satisfaction, importance, or ease of use, for example:

✔︎ How easy was it to complete the task? ✔︎ How do users rate the importance of factors, such as delivery speed? ✔︎ Satisfaction level with the product or a specific feature. ✔︎ Impressions of the interface and emotions while using it.
What to consider when using the method:
The optimal scale length is 5 or 7 points.
Many scales in a row tire respondents.
Labels are required — without them, people interpret numbers differently.
A scale shows subjective opinion, not actual behavior.

For serious research, such as satisfaction, importance of factors, CSI, or NPS, it is better to use numeric scales. Emoji can be left for quick reactions or “light surveys,” where engagement is more important than precise data.
What results you will get:
Average score
Overall statistics
The respondent evaluates several factors at once on a scale, for example from “not important at all” to “very important” or from “poor” to “excellent.”
The method helps compare several parameters at the same time and see what matters most to users. For example:

✔︎ Which product characteristics influence choice the most? ✔︎ How well does the product perform different tasks, such as speed, simplicity, and reliability? ✔︎ How do users rate different aspects of the experience: navigation, visual style, loading speed?
What to consider when using the method:
Do not overload the question: 5–10 factors is optimal.
Make sure factor wording is clear and does not overlap.
Use the same scale for comparison.
The method does not explain “why” — for that, you need interviews.
Look at statistical significance: the difference in means should be larger than the confidence interval to treat it as real rather than random.
What results you will get:
Factor ranking
An open question is one of the simplest and at the same time most difficult ways to get information. It helps:
In surveys, when the topic is new and you do not yet know how to classify possible answers.
In tests, to learn after a scenario how a person explains their actions, impressions, or difficulties.
The difficulty is that respondents quickly get tired of writing detailed answers. Use open questions selectively — only where it is truly important to hear the user’s “live voice.”

In Pathway, you can enable AI follow-up: it will ask clarifying questions that you would ask manually and help collect deeper answers. All text and voice responses are automatically transcribed and added to the report.
What to consider when using the method
Do not overload respondents: 1–2 open questions per study are enough.
Place the open question at the end of the block, after the main choice or rating.
Ask for an example: this adds specificity and helps interpretation.
What results you will get:
Text answers from which you can identify themes, patterns, and insights.

Through Pathway’s AI summary, you can get a summary of answers with links to specific cases, as well as automatically generated answer themes with a detailed summary of how many people mentioned each theme.

The respondent is given a question with several answer options. They can choose several that apply to them. The method helps understand which options are truly meaningful and what the choice is based on, for example:

✔︎ Which payment or delivery methods are used most often. ✔︎ Which communication channels are considered convenient: email, messengers, calls. ✔︎ Which barriers and difficulties occur more often than others. ✔︎ Which information sources help people decide about a product or service.
What to consider when using the method:
Options should be clear and not overlap with each other.
Do not use multiple choice without a qualitative base: first collect options through interviews, desk research, or open questions, and only then validate them in a closed list.
Avoid overly long lists: 6–10 items is optimal. If there are more than 12–15 options, group them or use filters, for example first category selection, then clarification.
For sensitive topics, such as income or health, phrase questions carefully and add a “Prefer not to answer” option.
Use an “Other” option so the respondent does not get stuck if they cannot find a suitable answer.
What results you will get:
Quantitative distribution across options.
Comments in the “Other” option.

children.title: Additional settings: when and why to use them

blocks.text: These settings help make surveys and tests more comfortable for respondents and more accurate for you. Below is a short overview of how and when to use each setting.
Optional question. The respondent can skip it if they do not have experience or are not ready to answer.
Suitable for sensitive topics: income, health, family status.
When a person may not know what the question refers to — for example, “night delivery.”
If there are too many optional questions, the answers will be incomplete.
An optional question helps retain participants. A person does not get stuck on difficult topics and does not abandon the test.
Allow selecting multiple options. Use this when the respondent may have several correct answers. This makes the data fuller and closer to real experience.
Examples of questions where multiple selection is suitable: “Which payment methods do you use?” “What difficulties have you faced?”
Shuffle answer order. If the list has logic, for example “18–24,” “25–34,” keep the order. If there is no logic, enable shuffling. This helps:
Reduce position bias: frequent selection of the first items
Prevent mechanical answers
Improve data accuracy

children.title: What results you can generally get from unmoderated research

blocks.text: Success rate: In unmoderated research, it is easy to see how many users reached the result and how many did not. A good scenario-test success rate means everyone completed it. Three out of four users is a decent result, but if there are several variants, you need to look at each success rate and check significance. We explain how to calculate it here → Efficiency: How much time a person needs to complete the task. In scenario tests, a long time points to difficulties completing scenarios. For first-click tests, I do not recommend treating time as the key metric; focus on success rate.

Problems: If users did not complete the task or spent a lot of time, you need to analyze the reasons — the problems they encountered. Path analysis and video review will help. Feature memorability: Within one test, you can check how well users remember how to use a feature. First ask them to complete the scenario of turning the feature on, and after some time — the scenario of turning it off, to compare how quickly and confidently participants handle the task. Satisfaction: To understand how perceived complexity matched real complexity. Experience, opinion, attitude: With multiple-choice questions, scales, matrices, and open fields, you can learn how people perceive the product, evaluate the experience, and relate to different solutions.

children.title: Checklist for checking tasks and questions in unmoderated research

blocks.text: Use this checklist to quickly make sure tasks are clear to respondents. Below you can read more about each point and see examples.
Add context before the task. Without a couple of lines about the situation, a person will not understand what to look for and why. Their behavior will be random.
Find a gift on the website that you think would be suitable. You want to buy a birthday gift for a colleague. Find a gift on the website that you think would be suitable.
In Pathway, the “Context” feature lets you immerse the respondent in a situation in advance without showing them the task.
One idea in one question. Do not combine two questions into one — the respondent will answer only part of it or get confused.
Which screen option seems more convenient to you, and which one would you choose? Which screen option seems more convenient to you? Which option would you choose?
Keep wording short. Long and overloaded phrases make perception harder and make respondents lose their place.
Choose which icon, in your subjective opinion and based on the overall page context, seems more appropriate. Which icon seems more appropriate to you?
Use real scenarios. Tasks should sound like familiar human actions, not like interface descriptions.
Choose the product card whose snippet seems most attractive to you. Which of the options seems more attractive to you?
Avoid unrealistic roles. Do not place people in situations far from their experience — this reduces the reliability of results.
For an audience that is not accountants: “Imagine you are looking for a job as an accountant.”
Minimize open questions. In unmoderated tests, open answers provide less data and are harder to analyze. It is better to use scales or answer options.
Where do you order specialists? What do you think of Profi.ru? (method: question) Where do you order specialists? (method: answer options) + “Rate Profi.ru on a scale from 1 to 10.”
Do not name interface elements. If you mention a button or link by name, the person will start looking for exactly that element rather than solving the task.
If the button on the screen is called “add to cart”: “What will you do to add it to the cart?” Imagine you want to buy this product. What will you do?
Check neutral wording. Questions should not contain evaluative words.
Find a convenient way to place an order. Find a way to place an order.

chapters.title: Audience: how to choose it and calculate the right number

blocks.text: To get results you can work with later, you need to understand whose answers will be useful.

It is important to consider respondents’ experience and behavioral factors. If you are building a competitor to “1C,” you will need accountants; if you are building a ticket-purchase aggregator, you need to recruit people who travel often.
Demographic factors such as age, geography, gender, and so on can also matter if you know that users from different demographic groups behave differently.
Most often, you can discuss the audience with the product manager.
Below, we will look in detail at how to focus on the right segment and invite its representatives to take part in the study.

children.title: How to define audience segments for research

blocks.text: A user’s history and context affect their experience with our product. For example, if you are building a service for delivering medicine from pharmacies, the needs and habits of older people will differ strongly from how younger people behave when buying medication. Product decisions, marketing campaigns, and other activities should be aligned with the different experiences of your customers, so it is important to conduct research with relevant representatives of your user groups.
When defining the target audience for a study, you need to answer two important questions:

Which criteria describe the group whose answers will be relevant? When defining criteria, consider: demographics such as age, gender, and region; income level; experience using our product, such as active users, newcomers, and others; experience using competitors. If you are planning a general study of audience needs or pains, or if the opinion of your typical user is enough, you can use broad criteria for your target audience, such as region and age, plus basic activity, or choose the most valuable and promising segment that brings more profit.
Do we need the experience of one group or several groups?
To decide, think about:
How fragmented your target audience is: whether you focus only on men aged 35–50 with average income, or whether you also target younger and older customers.

How different your direct competitors are: the different experience of their users will affect how they see your product, and it may be important to learn different opinions, for example WB and Ozon customers and their perception of Yandex Market.
Whether the results will be used only to attract new customers, retain existing ones, or both. If both, you will need to compare the perception of these groups.
You can read about how many respondents you need when working with several groups in the chapter “How many respondents you need” →.
What the size of the user pool can tell you

During sample selection, you will most likely be able to estimate the size of the user pool that matches the criteria for the segment you need. Sometimes it can be very small, for example 20–30 people, and sometimes, on the contrary, very large, for example around 100,000 people.

If the pool is very small, consider relaxing the search criteria to recruit a relevant number of respondents. Beyond its importance for recruiting, this signal may mean that the overall population of users with the target criteria is so small that it may not make sense to treat them as significant representatives of your target audience; you may need to reconsider your focus. Also, the smaller the pool, the longer recruitment will take.

If the pool is very large, on the contrary, consider whether it would be useful to specify the criteria more precisely, focus on a particular user group, or study differences inside it so that you do not end up with conclusions that are too general and blurred. However, when building a sample from your “own” users, a large pool also sends a positive signal: the size of the group means it is important within your target audience, so its opinion matters for product development.
Recruit respondents “from inside” or “from outside”?
When planning the research design, you need to decide whether you can get the needed answers from your users or whether you need to find an audience that is not familiar with your product.
This is easy to determine by asking yourself key questions related to the business problem of the study:

What knowledge do we want to gain? If the goal is to understand perception and work patterns specifically with our product, invite existing users. If you need to learn about needs and pains in the broader domain, it is worth adding an external audience to the sample.

Which metric do we want to influence using the research results? If we want to increase activity among current users, it is important to learn about their experience, for example if we aim to reduce churn rate. If we want to attract new customers, it may be important to learn about the needs of an external audience.

Are we studying an update to existing functionality, or planning to create something new? In the first case, it is important to check how already acquired customers will react to the new interface or experience. When something new is being designed, it may be important to hear how this already works in the industry for users of other services and solutions.

Do you have clear competitors, and does experience using them matter? The presence of other strong players in the market means that users may already have established patterns and habits based on best practices from other products. The influence of this experience is important to consider so you avoid mistakes and build a competitive product; to do that, you need to talk to customers of other services.

Does the product have enough active internal users to answer the research questions? If your service has only recently started developing, the active customer base may be too small for relevant results. In that case, an external audience or a mixed sample, with some internal and some external users, can help.

Pro tip: A useful practice for qualitative research is a list of “warm respondents.” You can build it from the surplus of people who agreed to participate during recruitment, or create an entry point in the product where people who want to participate in the future can leave their contacts. Such a list helps recruit respondents faster or find a replacement in an emergency.
To invite users who are not familiar with your product to a study, you can use the following options:

Recruiting agency services. Companies that specialize in selecting respondents for research in specific countries and regions can recruit respondents for you end to end. This option is convenient and frees up your time, but it is significantly more expensive than the others.

Recruiting respondents through the unauthorized area of your service. If you have tools for setting up pop-up surveys on product landing pages that unauthorized users can reach, you can invite interested people to take part in your study. The downside is low conversion and sample bias: participants will already be somewhat interested in your product because they reached its landing page.

Manual respondent search in thematic communities or other places. This is the most time-consuming and labor-intensive option. However, it can help when you need representatives of a very narrow segment that is hard to reach in other ways, for example owners of private dairy farms in southern Russia.

Respondent panels and the capabilities of survey and unmoderated-testing platforms. These services often let you distribute a research link to your own audience and recruit external respondents through their panel. This option is often optimal because it helps achieve high conversion quickly at a reasonable price.

For example, Pathway offers more than 120 parameters for finding respondents inside the platform. This helps you find research audiences from Russia, Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Armenia, Georgia, and other CIS countries in just a few clicks. On average, Pathway customers save around 800,000 rubles per year on respondent recruitment.
Example of external-audience recruiting settings in the Pathway interface. ALT

Pro tip: Sometimes an “external” view of your own customers can bring unexpected insights. For example, this happened to the VkusVill team. They decided to roll out one study to a new segment and use respondents brought by Pathway, while making sure through a qualifying question that they also shopped at VkusVill. Even though these were the same VkusVill customers, their user experience was slightly different. They behaved differently from the segment the team usually sent surveys to. This made it possible to look at a familiar audience from a new angle and see other behavior patterns. You can read more about this case on vc.ru.
Finding the right audience for a study can be difficult, and it becomes even more complicated when researching a B2B audience.
Let’s recall how these two audiences differ:

B2B, or Business-to-Business, is a business model in which a company sells its product or offers services to other organizations for work-related purposes. B2C, or Business-to-Consumer, offers products and services to individuals, consumers who use the service for personal needs.
The overall populations of user segments in B2B are usually significantly smaller than in B2C.

Example: Imagine a product for cafe owners, such as an online menu, and a product for visitors of those venues, such as an app with a map of specialty coffee shops in the city. The target audience of the B2B product for cafe owners will be smaller than the B2C product for visitors because there are far fewer entrepreneurs who have opened a coffee shop than people who go to such places.

One product can have both a B2B and a B2C audience at the same time. Using the same online menu for cafes as an example, the business needs to make sure the product is interesting and clear for cafe owners (B2B) and convenient for the end customer (B2C). Such products are often called B2B2C: business-to-business-to-consumer.

Pro tip: If your audience is mostly B2B, think about how the final B2C customer of your audience influences it. For example, in the B2B product Ostrovok, whose users are travel agents, the team decided to interview B2C users — clients of those agencies. One of the main hypotheses was that the habits and preferences of end customers could significantly influence the needs of the travel agents themselves. This hypothesis was confirmed.

Despite the difficulties, a B2B audience is reachable. Julian Della Mattia, founder of the From Finders to Builders podcast, and Ksenia Zhebrovskaya, Brand and Business Development Manager at Pathway, share techniques that make B2B respondent recruitment easier:

For internal recruitment, collaborate with support, customer success, sales, or client curator teams. They build and maintain relationships with customers, so they can definitely help you attract participants.

Attend industry events. Join online communities, go to meetups and conferences related to your field, where you can meet your target audience. Be human, open, and transparent: talk to people, explain in detail what you plan to do and how you would like to involve them.

If recruiting is very difficult, think about what you can change in your approach. This may be the participant selection criteria, the reward you offer, or the invitation text itself. Treat all of these as hypotheses and test them one by one. Sometimes changing the wording of the invitation is enough to completely change the situation.

Be creative. Ksenia gives an example from her own experience. When the team needed to study the Hong Kong market with a Chinese-speaking audience, they hired a talented marketing manager from the startup community. She used her contacts and brought exactly the sample they needed for in-person focus groups in their office. This case shows perfectly how personal connections and nonstandard thinking can lead to successful recruiting.

Identify the recruiting panels most relevant to your target audience. For example, Pathway has access to one of the largest respondent panels in Russia and the CIS countries. If you are interested in an international market, Pathway’s cooperation with Tandems, TestingTime, and PolarInsight may help. Tandems are valued for recruiting respondents from Brazil, TestingTime works well with participants from the Netherlands, and for studies focused on the UK, good options include FieldworkHub or People4Research.

Broaden the search focus. Sometimes suitable B2B respondents can be found among a wider audience, even B2C. For example, if your product helps teams manage processes and build a transparent corporate culture, many B2C users may have experience with similar tools at work. In that case, everything comes down to effective screening or even automatic targeting on platforms like Prolific or Respondent.io.
Unfortunately, there is no simple answer, so first we will explain what it depends on.

Depending on the goals, research can be qualitative or quantitative. In short: qualitative research is used to find problems and generate solutions; quantitative research is used to test hypotheses and get a statistically significant answer.
The required number of respondents will differ for each type. We explain how to calculate it below.

If you are running unmoderated qualitative research, for example analyzing user interaction with a prototype, collecting answers to open questions, or card-sorting results, in most cases we recommend 15–20 respondents. If you need to compare the experience of different user groups, this number is valid for one group, for example 15 × 3 groups = 45 respondents.
Why this number?
In quantitative research, we test a hypothesis on a large number of respondents to get a statistically significant result.
“Statistically significant” means that the result will be true not only for the people who participated in the test, but for all other users as well.

Example: Imagine that 10,000 people use our service. We took 100 people and showed them a button for a new feature. All 100 understood what the button does. But will the remaining 9,900 people understand it? That is a big question, because 100 people is only 1%, and maybe we simply got lucky. This is where statistical significance helps. Let’s look at a few terms — they will help calculate the number of respondents.
Population — all people who belong to your product audience. For example, everyone who shops online, or everyone registered as self-employed.
Sample size — it is impossible to survey the entire audience, so the concept of a sample and its size exists. Statistical methods help generalize results from a sample to the population.
Margin of error — a possible deviation between the results obtained from testing the sample and the real values in the population.

Example using the same service: Population, meaning all users, = 10,000. Sample = 100 people. Suppose 50 people in the sample understood what the new button does. A margin of error of ±10% means that for all 10,000 users you can say: between 4,000 and 6,000 of them will also understand the meaning of the new button. In short: if 50% of the sample understood it, then with a ±10% margin of error, 40% to 60% of the population will also understand it. The number of respondents is always a difficult question. On the one hand, the more respondents you have, the closer the result will be to reality. On the other hand, you will have to spend more resources attracting respondents. That is why answering this question always means balancing research cost and result accuracy.

children.title: How many respondents you need for quantitative research

blocks.text: In general, we recommend recruiting 95–100 people for each quantitative UX study. The maximum possible margin of error with this sample will be about 10%.

For the overwhelming majority of cases, this will be enough: in unmoderated research, it does not make sense to aim for precision of a few percent or to detect a very small difference when comparing several variants. A/B testing is better suited for that.

It is important to note that using a different number of respondents, usually from 30 to 400 people, is not a mistake. It is simply another balance point between result accuracy and research cost. In the next section, we will look at how this works in more detail.

children.title: How to calculate the number of respondents for quantitative research

blocks.text: The number of respondents depends on the maximum acceptable margin of error for the results relative to the population.
To simplify, the acceptable margin of error is the one you are comfortable with — remember the balance between accuracy and research cost.
A statistically significant minimum metric change is the difference between test results at which you can consider the results to differ for the population as well.
The table below shows how margin of error and minimum change depend on the number of respondents in the sample.
Sample size. Margin of error. 30 — 18%. 43 — 15%. 96 — 10%. 384 — 5%.
To calculate the values in the table, we used a 95% significance level and assumed a large population — more than 100,000 people.
Example
You can change these values and calculate the sample size with another margin of error and confidence level using our calculator:
The question of whether testing participants should be rewarded often comes up in product-community conversations. The answer is clear: yes, and here is why.
Higher participation conversion. A reward motivates respondents to participate in research in exchange for material compensation.

Sample normalization. Active respondents who are willing to share opinions for free are most often at the extremes of the sample: very satisfied, very upset, or with a lot of free time. Feedback from such users cannot reliably reflect the situation in the population, which includes busy or quiet users with neutral experiences. Financial motivation helps attract them too.

It formalizes the relationship between researcher and respondent. Participation in research is a kind of work that requires concentration and involvement in the process. Knowing that the participant will receive a reward makes them a more responsible respondent and helps the researcher feel entitled to ask many questions and follow-ups.
It communicates respect for the user. Paying for spent time and useful information is good practice that helps strengthen the company’s reputation and can even increase customer loyalty.
What if we cannot pay for participation?

In young products or under budget constraints, it may be impossible to provide rewards for research participation. You can still talk to respondents in this case: research in imperfect conditions is better than no research at all.
To minimize sample distortion in this situation:

Try to recruit participants proactively, inviting them through online mailings or direct contact, so you reach interested respondents who are not necessarily actively seeking communication with you themselves.
Control key demographic parameters and product-activity data using analytics or qualification questions. This will help make sure you are getting a balanced sample.
If possible, expand the candidate pool, for example from 1,000 to 5,000–6,000 people, to compensate for the drop in conversion without rewards.

children.title: What to give for participating in a study

blocks.text: If you plan to launch an unmoderated study on the Pathway platform and find participants through partner user panels such as OnIn, Tiburon, and others, this question will not require additional thought. Respondents who complete tests through Pathway receive rewards automatically, in an amount calculated according to market standards depending on the audience.
However, if you plan to reward respondents yourself or distribute a test created in Pathway to your internal audience, you can consider these options:
Certificates for popular regional marketplaces. A universal reward that almost every participant can use. It works best for longer interviews or moderated tests.
Merch. Branded gifts are valuable because they are unique and create a sense of belonging to your product. If the merch is also useful, it becomes an excellent reward.

A promo code for your service. If your service provides widely used services, such as buying electronics or booking hotels, a large discount or the ability to get something for free is a good incentive. This option works well for quantitative surveys and unmoderated tests because it is easy to implement online.

For the premium segment: experience gifts and compliments. High-income audiences are difficult to motivate with money, and your study will most likely attract the most curious and open among them. Such respondents can be interested in unusual, status-oriented gifts or compliments, for example sending a fruit basket or assigning a special status in your product.

To calculate the reward amount for moderated and other sessions, you can use one of the online calculators that account for region, recruitment difficulty, and participation effort. For example, Ethnio.

chapters.title: Prototypes: what types exist and how to prepare them for testing

blocks.text: To test an interface, you will need material: a prototype, static screens, or images. In product teams, prototypes are usually provided by designers, but sometimes researchers create them themselves if they have the experience and the right tools.

To quickly prepare materials and adapt them for testing, a technical brief will be useful. It is usually created by the researcher, who already has an approximate research design in mind. The technical brief should think through and describe:

The required screens and the connections between them. If the materials are static, which exact screens or images are needed? If we show a prototype, from which screen will the flow start and where will it end? Which transitions are needed from one step to another, will they be linear, or will we allow users to go back?
Functionality. Do we need an interactive prototype or a static screen? Which elements need to be active? Which actions does the respondent need to perform?
Target action. What main action are we checking, for example placing an order? Which actions will be considered indicators that the task was completed correctly?

Element states. Many interfaces have elements that change status, such as color, enabled state, or activity. To help respondents correctly understand what is happening on screens during testing, you need to think through the state of key elements, for example toggles on or off, buttons active or inactive.

Data on screens. If you are testing an interface with personal data, products, or other objects, think through what they will display so they are suitable for different respondents while creating a relatively relevant situation for each of them, for example account balance, dish options, locations.

If you already have a prototype or know that the test material will be a prototype, check its readiness using our checklist. And if you still need to decide what to show respondents and at which stage of the project to start testing, let’s figure it out together.

To get feedback on an interface concept, you can start researching it at the earliest stage of idea formation. Moreover, the earlier you start, the more confidently you can move forward in designing the user experience.
Testing material can include:
Static screens
Interactive prototypes, from low to high fidelity
Test environments and beta versions of the product
A live product version, production

Pro tip: Another nonstandard way to get user data for interface design is co-creation, where the client creates or edits a layout together with you in real time according to their vision. Such a session can be organized offline, by drawing on paper, or in online tools. This format helps understand the user’s mental model and generate new ideas.
Different levels of prototype and material detail correspond to different stages of concept development.
The scale on which the desired level of detail grows can be illustrated like this:

This means that at the idea stage, you can test very rough prototypes or static screens that show the concept to understand whether you are moving in the right direction. However, the closer you are to releasing new functionality, the more important it is to be confident that everything works as expected throughout the whole flow and that corner cases are considered.

Example: If you are creating a new app for a music streaming service, one example of material for an early test could be a simple static home screen. With it, you can check whether a potential user sees all the important blocks they care about. After that, you can design the full interface with more confidence. But before moving to development, another testing stage with a detailed interactive prototype will be useful to make sure the user flow works without issues.

Pathway lets you use static images, detailed prototypes, a link to an already working website, and even record a respondent’s actions in the browser as test material. This makes our service an ideal tool for testing at every stage of your project development.

children.title: Specifics of prototypes for unmoderated testing

blocks.text: The unmoderated testing format determines how prototypes should be prepared. Here is what this means:

Start point and flow length. In moderated testing, we can afford to start far away, for example directly from the product home page, even if we are testing functionality hidden in the profile, so we can also check how intuitive the path to the target action is for the respondent. In unmoderated testing, however, the participant usually spends less time with the prototype, and unnecessary flow length increases the risk that they never reach the target area. If it is not critical for the research design, you can start 1–2 screens closer to the target area, for example directly in the profile.

Number of degrees of freedom. In moderated testing, the researcher can gently guide the respondent if they go the wrong way and bring them back to the target flow. When a person works with the prototype alone, you need to minimize the chance of getting lost or stuck: think through clear transitions without dead ends and limit prototype interactivity to only the necessary actions.

Stability level. For unmoderated testing, the prototype needs to be carefully checked for possible errors. Without a moderator, the user will not be able to report that the prototype broke, and you will not be able to fix it quickly.

If you want to compare several prototype versions, for example to test flow completion with two visual-solution variants, you must strictly ensure that only the control variable changes between versions, for example different profile icons. Otherwise, the results cannot be considered relevant because it will be impossible to filter out the influence of other changes. This rule applies to both moderated and unmoderated testing.

children.title: Checklist: how to prepare a prototype and what to do after the test

blocks.text: Before the study
Create a prototype for all tested devices: iOS, Android if there are meaningful differences, mobile and desktop web, and software-based applications.
Check that the prototype matches the respondent’s device.
Move the prototype into a separate project, not just a new page. This reduces the risk of opening the wrong layout.
Provide link access without requiring app installation. Make sure the link is available to everyone.
Make names, prices, locations, payment methods, and other information inside the prototype realistic. Milk cannot cost 1,000 rubles, and “John Doe” will not make the test feel realistic.
Remove extra screens or branches that are not related to the test. If some were created for demo purposes, delete them.
Make sure all tasks and screens form a logical, consistent story and match the research objectives.
Eliminate dead ends. The user should always be able to continue the scenario when moving from one task to another, even if they chose the wrong path.

Make all fields clickable and empty, without autofill. Pre-filled fields confuse users and make them think something was added automatically. If there are many fields to fill in and you need to test whether they are clear, Figma may not be suitable. In that case, consider other prototyping tools, such as Axure.

Hide hotspots, the clickable-area highlights. Remove them before creating the link. If you cannot disable active zones in Figma, ask the respondent to do so before the test starts and provide clear instructions. You can also edit the link by adding “&hotspot-hints=0” to the end or changing the value from “1” to “0.”

Check that the URL does not reveal the purpose of the test or the company name. For example, “onboarding_check” is bad; “problem17” is good. If brand anonymity matters, remove any mention of your company from the link.

Make sure the URL does not reveal what you are testing. For example, “onboarding_check” is bad; “problem17” is good. If you need brand anonymity, the link should not contain a reference to your company.
Split heavy prototypes into parts and number them. Services like Zoom often struggle with the load.
For unmoderated testing, split the prototype into several parts, each dedicated to a specific task. The researcher can help you define what those tasks should be.
Add the ability to go back at any stage. This lets users repeat their steps if they get confused, and lets you or the researcher ask additional questions.
If you compare several screens, the differences should be only in the tested element.
If needed, insert intermediate screens for context, for example “two months later.”
Additional checklist for international products
The interface should match the country where the test is being conducted. Pay special attention to the displayed banks, names, and country.
All prototype text should be in the appropriate language, depending on the test language.
After the study is complete
Adjust access settings or delete the prototype. If you delete it, make sure the prototype was in a separate project and was moved back to the main one after completion.

chapters.title: Checklist: what else to think about before launch

blocks.text: You know what you want to learn and why the business needs it.
You understand what you want to learn. In other words, the main research goal has been formulated.
There is a decision path based on the results. You know what you or the team will do in any outcome.
You are sure that research is what you need, not an A/B test or analytics.
The method matches the goal: scenario test, first click, preference test, scale, matrix, and others.
