Questions and tasks have been checked against the checklist.
Show the study to colleagues on your team. They may have valuable comments.

Enable pilot testing to get feedback. It is recommended to plan a pilot usability test — a rehearsal with an employee from your organization. Make sure the pilot participant was not involved in preparing the usability test; otherwise, you risk false consensus. You need a person with roughly the same knowledge level as an external participant, otherwise the pilot will be inaccurate. A pilot test helps improve the process, refine tasks and questions, and identify any problems in the current testing setup. What seems simple to you may be difficult for participants because they have relatively little information to work with, so a pilot session is essential.

You need to understand when the data is needed and why that timing matters. This affects method choice, makes it possible to plan research, and helps you stay in the business rhythm. Or, if there is no rush, it helps you plan properly and work comfortably.

Pro tip: User data is needed at the right time and place. Research results usually have a “window of opportunity” when they can bring maximum benefit and there is enough time to implement them. For example, discovery interviews will bring important knowledge for product-strategy planning, while testing a prototype of a new feature is needed before it goes to production. Consider these timeframes and think through deadlines.

children.title: Who can be responsible for what: product managers, designers, editors, analysts, and marketers

blocks.text: Think about who you will do the study with, who will be responsible for what, and what your own responsibility is. Research is always teamwork. It is important to involve people: if someone participates in business decisions but is not connected to the research, that becomes a problem rather than an advantage. And if resources are limited, think about whom you can ask for support.
Basic areas of responsibility are usually distributed like this:

The product manager owns the task and then makes the business decision based on the research. They immerse you in the context, explain the business problem and their expectations, share questions and hypotheses, study the data and report, and discuss thoughts after the study.

The designer owns the design and makes decisions about it. They formulate questions and hypotheses for the test, define expectations, prepare prototypes and monitor their quality, participate in the research, and make design changes based on the results. Their involvement in the study may vary. When it comes to usability tests, designers increasingly run them themselves. This is convenient: the researcher does not need to be immersed in the details of the solution, the launch is faster, and the designer can make changes immediately without reports and retellings. The researcher’s role in this process is to provide tools, train the team, and help with conclusions if needed. But responsibility for the brief, launch, analysis, and decisions remains with the designer. “I am always happy when designers or product managers run tests on Pathway themselves. It reduces the time needed to transfer context and usually greatly increases motivation because the team sees feedback on their work. The researcher’s task is to provide the tool in advance and describe a clear process and guides so it is clear which methodologies to use in which cases and for which tasks. During the test, the researcher answers questions, helps with methodology and complex moments, and reviews the test and result analysis. Research democratization enables the research team to work more effectively, and design and product teams to interact more with user feedback.” Marina Suslova, Head of Research, Zvuk.

The editor owns the text and makes decisions about it. They formulate questions and hypotheses, define expectations, write copy, and make sure that when variants are compared, only the tested attribute changes. After the study, they make edits. Like designers, editors can research their own texts themselves.

The analyst owns work with data and helps strengthen research conclusions. They can be a source of hypotheses and questions, and motivate the product manager to check behavior visible in product analytics. After the study, they evaluate release results, enrich insights with analytics data, and help ground conclusions at larger volumes, which makes prioritization easier.

The marketer owns product communication and can be a source of research tasks. For example, NPS results may show that the app’s inconvenience reduces people’s willingness to share it with friends. This insight becomes a reason to run research together with the product manager. Another common case is a drop in landing-page registration conversion. Marketing usually tracks this metric and may request a check of whether users really understand who the product is for and whether they find everything they need on the page to register.

The composition of research participants can vary greatly. In some companies, it is important to involve Sales; in others, developers or AI/ML specialists participate more actively. It all depends on context: roles may differ, but the main thing is that the team helps each other and moves the research forward together.

The company’s maturity level and available resources determine how actively the researcher, or the person who has taken on the study, can be involved in research, and what role the rest of the team takes.

For example, if one researcher works with five teams at once and a product manager wants to talk to users every week and get new insights, the researcher physically cannot take this task fully on. In that case, the product manager can act as the interviewer, while the researcher provides methodological support and develops quality.

chapters.title: What to consider before drawing conclusions

blocks.text: 1. Perfect accuracy does not exist. Results are affected by the method, sample, and data-collection approach. But with a careful approach, distortions are minimal — and the level of reliability will be more than enough to make a confident business decision.

2. Do not treat research as a source of a ready-made answer. It will provide validated facts that help the team make a balanced decision. But to make that decision, you still need to check against business goals and think carefully about the best way to meet user needs. Figuratively speaking, sometimes when a user asks for a blue button, their real problem may be solved by a green banner.

Example: A marketplace team was choosing a name for a new delivery type and decided to test several options: describe the delivery format and ask people to come up with a name for it, then choose the most suitable one from a list of ready options. To expand the list of options, alongside other, more creative names, the survey included “Convenient delivery.” Because of its cognitive simplicity, this option received the most votes, and similar phrases often appeared in answers where respondents were asked to invent a name themselves. However, from the perspective of branding and product positioning — if we call one delivery type convenient, does that mean the others are inconvenient? — the team could not choose the most popular survey option and considered the names that placed second and third. In an ideal world, “Convenient delivery” should have been excluded from testing.
3. One study is often not enough. To make a balanced and well-founded decision, you usually need different methods and several research rounds.

children.title: How to analyze unmoderated research results

blocks.text: Look at comments or detailed respondent answers if you have them. Remove answers that are clearly random or meaningless. Count how many comments point to problems with the question or layout. If there are many such answers, more than 10%, fix the issue and run the test again. Analyze open comments and create a classification of the most frequent opinions or complaints. Pathway’s AI summary will help you quickly orient yourself around the main themes and sentiment in open comments.
Evaluate numerical metrics if you received them from the study.
Calculate significance if the test compared several variants — this will help you estimate the probability that differences exist not only in the sample, but also in the population.
Estimate the margin of error so you can project the result to the population.

If the study was conducted with a prototype, analyze how users interacted with your interface. Pathway offers convenient tools for a deeper look into respondent patterns: watching user session recordings helps you understand how they behaved while working with the prototype and where difficulties appeared; screen-transition maps help reveal the dominant flow; heatmaps show which interface elements attracted the most interest from respondents and seemed interactive. Example of a heatmap in the Pathway interface that can provide additional insights about your interface. ALT
After the data has been processed and the conclusions have been formulated, it is time to apply the insights in practice:

Document the results. You should be able to return to the findings — to reuse them, grow your knowledge base about users, and remember which product decisions were based on them. Choose a suitable format: mature teams usually have a report library, while for quick tests it may be enough to document findings directly in the Figma project. The main thing is to give colleagues access and keep everything in a shared workspace.

Share the findings with the team. The format depends on the scale of the study and the team culture: it may be a presentation, a short messenger post, or an update in Confluence. The goal is the same: interested colleagues and requesters should be able to use the insights in their work and understand what data informed the decisions.

Define the next steps. What decisions need to be made based on the results: change the interface, choose a direction, approve a release? If it is a small unmoderated test, the decision can be made immediately. If the study is complex and strategic, bring the team together to discuss the next steps.
Move to action. Once the direction is chosen, the key stage begins: implementing changes based on user data. Each team member takes responsibility for their own part of the work.
Evaluate the effect. After changes are implemented, analyze their impact on key metrics. This step shows the real value of research and helps adjust the approach if the result is below expectations.

Choose the necessary depth of work with results depending on the situation. For example, if you work in a startup without a dedicated research team and run quick tests to decide on interface solutions, it may be enough to document the main findings in your working tools, such as Figma, and summarize them in working groups, such as a company messenger. If research work in your company is significantly developed, if you have a user knowledge repository, or if you ran a large discovery study, it will likely require deeper processing, live presentations, and even workshops.
Below, we will discuss what to do when you need deeper work with research results. This may be the case if:
You ran a large discovery study with many hypotheses and many insights
You are building a user knowledge base and it is important to document research findings in a structured way
The findings have strategic importance and major product-wide decisions must be made based on them
You work in a research team whose job is not only to quickly validate interface decisions, but also to help the product develop based on user experience overall.

The most important thing is that the research artifact directly answers the hypotheses and questions set at the beginning. To avoid missing anything, open the briefing notes or the hypothesis document and cover each point in the report one by one.

To make the report clear and transparent, write it as if it will be read by someone who did not participate in the project. Imagine explaining the work to a colleague from accounting: they know your product, but they are not immersed in the details. Will they understand why you ran the study? Will the abbreviations be clear? Will they be able to imagine what the tested interface looked like?
A report can be prepared in any convenient format: slides, a Confluence or Notion page, or an online board. The main thing is to follow general structure principles. The most important ones are:
What you did: the study type and what was researched — this can also be reflected in the title
When: this can also be reflected in the title — it helps readers understand how current the study is
Why: describe the business problem, context, study goal, and hypotheses
What materials were tested: links to prototypes, clear screenshots of the interface when describing issues
Key findings and recommendations: place them at the beginning so the reader can quickly understand the main results
The main detail block with observations: it can be grouped by the hypotheses you tested
Next steps: what will happen next with the research results — this block can and should be updated after the results presentation
Your report structure may look like this: STRUCTURE DIAGRAM (TO DRAW):
Q2 2025 API… — Date / Month of the study + Title
Context of the study
Business Aim
Aims of the study
Hypotheses — hypotheses and research questions
Sample
Key outputs (not in the image, but make it a block slightly larger than Aims of the study) — key findings
Detailed information (not in the image, but make it the largest block on the page)
What’s next (not in the image, but make it a block slightly larger than Aims of the study) — next steps
In the right column:
Materials, including: research presentation, interview recordings (in our version, this can be replaced with “raw data”), tracker task, recording of the research presentation
Table of contents
When describing interface issues, be sure to show the interface itself, the problem area, and describe:
What exactly happened (the essence of the issue)
Issue severity
How it appeared during the test
Why this is a problem
DIAGRAM WITH A SAMPLE ISSUE DESCRIPTION (DRAW USING A SCREENSHOT EXAMPLE):
Section title, for example Checkout page
Brief issue summary, for example “users do not understand where to click to place an order”
Issue severity (not shown in the screenshot, but write briefly, for example “High severity”; you can highlight it in red)

A block describing the issue, for example: 8 out of 30 users could not complete the target action, which was clicking the “Place order” button, and spent about two minutes on average trying to find it in the interface before giving up.
Why this is a problem, for example: this issue may reduce conversion to the target action, placing an order.
The severity of a usability issue consists of three factors:
Frequency: how many respondents encountered it? How many times in one test?
Consequences: will users be able to handle it easily, or will the losses be significant?
Where it appears, or persistence: will users encounter the issue once, or will it keep bothering them constantly?
DIAGRAM FOR DETERMINING SEVERITY (DRAW IN THE GUIDE STYLE):
examples can be changed; they are written out

Pro-tip: When communicating research results, check them against the business strategy. Sometimes users may ask for functionality that the company physically cannot build or does not plan to implement yet according to its strategy. It is worth highlighting that the audience has a need, but pushing irrelevant ideas too insistently may be perceived as narrow-mindedness and can undermine trust.
A report is the main artifact, but not the only one produced by research. Depending on the situation, you may end up with about 7–9 useful project materials:
Research brief / tracker task
Research script: a guide with questions, a survey draft, or a task list
Research materials: prototypes, images
Original raw data: interview recordings, survey answers, or unmoderated test data
Report
Short presentation: slides
Video recording of your presentation
Post or email with brief results
Workspace for brainstorming or a workshop
Initiatives based on research results: tickets, tasks

When preparing the report, pay attention to linking these materials together and making it easy for the reader to access them. This will be useful both for understanding the study context and for you in the future, so you can quickly find and reuse existing artifacts or compare new data with them.

Depending on the timeline, the presentation can be held either before or after writing the detailed report. Sometimes a presentation with prepared slides can become both a way to share results and a reporting format.
The following steps will help the presentation achieve its goals and run smoothly:

Step 0 — Think about who should be present for the results discussion. In addition to requesters, adjacent teams and other colleagues may be interested in the study because your findings may affect them. To define the participant list, you can consult the study requester.

Step 1 — Start with the main point, then go into the details. Do not make listeners wait: state the main research findings right away, then explain what they mean. This will make it easier for meeting participants to understand what is happening and connect the results into a single picture.

Step 2 — Summarize and repeat the main points again. Human perception works in such a way that we remember best what we hear at the beginning and at the end. So at the end of your talk, briefly repeat the main findings.

Step 3 — Allow time for questions and discussion. A live discussion of the results will help you get valuable feedback on your study and immediately arrive at some ideas and initiatives based on the findings.

Step 4 — Plan the next steps. It is worth spending at least a little time documenting the next steps, even if it is only an agreement about when you will meet for the next discussion. This helps the team feel responsible for working with the results and facilitates implementation. You did not just listen and leave — you know exactly what you will do next and when.
Step 5 — Send a follow-up email or message. After the presentation, give colleagues a simple and clear path to the research materials and duplicate your agreements.
The visibility of a researcher’s work is an important factor in developing UX culture in a company. Think carefully about how and where you should highlight your results:

Identify the main touchpoints with stakeholders and the internal audience for your research, and bring the results there. These may be channels in the company messenger, regular syncs, or demo meetings. The more often colleagues hear about your projects, the more people will have access to useful data at the moment of important decisions.

Publish results in a way that makes them easy to share. The easier it is to distribute findings, the further they will travel. Use a short post with a link to the full report, cards with the main insights, and definitely publish them in channels open to most colleagues.

Plan publication so that the results appear where they are relevant. If the project you researched has a root document or a tracker task, it is useful to add a link to the results there. If you ran several related or sequential studies, make sure it is possible to move from one to another.
You can and should participate in implementing recommendations in the product and facilitate that work:

Run a workshop based on the research results. To help the team decompose, understand, and apply the findings, a workshop or brainstorming session based on a template you prepared may be useful. Its goal is to outline the next steps or product improvement initiatives based on research data.

Track the implementation status of recommendations. Set a control deadline — either separately for each project or a standard one for all — after which you return to the project owner for updates on whether your recommendations have been taken into work. This is one of the most labor-intensive processes and is not easy to set up, but it will raise your understanding of research impact on the product to a new level. You will be able to analyze which types of research are most applicable in practice, which ones produce meaningful impact, and which ones end up in a drawer. This understanding becomes the basis for improving the effectiveness of your work.

Quote: “In Ostrovok’s B2B research direction, we introduced a regular process for evaluating the impact of research projects on the product. Once a quarter, we meet and discuss whether recommendations from studies completed in the previous quarter were taken into work. If we learn that the recommendations were developed and taken into account, we document exactly how and whether metrics increased as a result of the changes. This evaluation helps us see how research actually helps the product, analyze why some projects sometimes end up in a drawer, and improve the practical applicability of our recommendations.” Darya Shakleeva, B2B Research Team Lead at Ostrovok

Become a “center of knowledge” about users. Your research should reach colleagues not only through texts and result publications, but also through your personal expertise. Take part in strategic planning and in work on the areas for which you ran tests and interviews. This will help protect the team from some mistakes and move forward more consciously.

Pro-tip [How to make effective research-based decisions] Help the team make decisions. The life of a study does not end with the presentation or publication of results. To make it easier for the team to decompose, understand, and implement the results, a workshop or brainstorming session based on a template you prepared may be useful. Its goal is to outline the next steps or product improvement initiatives based on research data.

children.title: AI in research: how it already helps

blocks.text: The rapid development of AI opens new opportunities to optimize processes in the UX researcher profession. Here are the main stages of research work where neural-network capabilities already save hours of routine work:

Checking the business problem. A quick way to understand how well it is formulated and what else should be clarified with the stakeholder. GPT prompt for evaluating a business problem and generating clarifying questions for stakeholders. Insert your business problem into “Business problem for analysis.”

Generating hypotheses. After formulating the main set of hypotheses during the project briefing with the requester, it is useful to ask a neural network to think with you about what else may be important to pay attention to during the study. The suggestions you get will complement the research focus and help you avoid missing nuances.
Choosing a methodology. LLMs can help select a set of research methods for testing your hypotheses and determine the type of data that will best answer the research question.

Searching for experimental data and research. Models with web search, such as Perplexity or newer GPT versions, can help find scientific articles or other research to support and strengthen your decisions.
Adjusting wording in surveys and interview scripts. LLMs help come up with more elegant ways to ask a question, check grammar, shorten sentences, and adjust tone of voice.

Analyzing open comments. With LLMs, you can analyze a large set of open comments, receiving ready-made findings or calculating the share of answers with certain keywords. Pathway offers this capability directly inside the platform when working with results analysis.

Translating answers from another language. Microsoft Office add-ins, Gemini in Google Spreadsheets, and other tools remove the language barrier when analyzing open comments. You can translate texts into the target language with AI in a couple of minutes directly in your working document.

Calculating data according to specified frameworks. Although neural networks are still not always flawless in data analysis, when given a calculation framework they can help calculate quantitative research results or analyze relationships in a dataset.

Quote: “After running a survey on promo-code mechanics among users, I used ChatGPT for Latent Class Analysis to identify hidden behavioral characteristics that group users into segments, or classes. I also asked it to list metrics that could be used to judge the accuracy and reliability of the model, so I could make sure the results were trustworthy. ChatGPT described the discovered segments, highlighted how each differed from the others, and calculated each segment’s representation in the sample. As a result, we identified the segment that responds best to promos and adjusted our mailings so that promo codes are sent to the people whose decision can truly be influenced by their presence.” Elizaveta Demchenko, Senior UX Researcher at Ostrovok

A 3D cube with clusters that Liza produced while working with GPT. Class 0 (black) is the densest and most compact. It is the central segment of the sample. Class 1 (blue) is separate from the others and is probably the most “special” in its characteristics because of greater dispersion. Class 2 (green) is clearly distinguishable, but closer to the main core, possibly showing a hybrid strategy toward promo codes, for example sometimes passive and sometimes active. ALT

Formulating conclusions. LLMs speed up data processing by helping interpret both quantitative and qualitative data. For example, tools such as Notebook LM or Breyta can help you find answers to research questions and formulate conclusions with specific quotes from interview transcripts.
Creating personas based on collected data. Neural networks do fairly well at forming user personas based on findings and results that you have already formulated.

Desk research and trend review. AI can help search for knowledge about current market trends and open data in your subject area. Perplexity and GPT’s “Search the web” feature are especially good at this.
Searching the knowledge base. Modern insight repository tools, such as Dovetail, or custom AI implementation in local knowledge bases can greatly simplify the search for relevant insights on a topic.

Quote: “We are constantly modifying our research knowledge base. As the number of researchers grew and the company split into business units, the insight base became too bulky and difficult to use. To make it easier to find answers to specific questions about users, we started testing a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) approach through Onyx, which connects to our Confluence. The model does not invent anything new; it extracts relevant fragments from the database and forms a connected answer with a link to the source. For us, this is a step toward turning the research base from an archive into a living decision-making tool.” Boris Yuzefpolsky, Head of UX Research at Ostrovok

Pro-tip: Neural-network models can not only help with routine work, but also immerse you in the subject area. When researching complex products or domains, especially in B2B services, you may encounter specific terminology and concepts that are interpreted differently from region to region. When analyzing free-form survey comments or interview results, you can ask a neural network to help you understand what the respondent meant and how to interpret their thoughts in a particular context — most of the time, this works very well.
In addition to well-known standalone products such as ChatGPT or Perplexity, many companies are integrating AI into their tools, making researchers’ lives significantly easier.
For example, Pathway can help you quickly review respondent answers with an AI summary or ask study participants follow-up questions based on the text they entered in an open-comment field.
SCREENSHOTS AND/OR LINKS
Pathway functionality that lets you ask study participants follow-up questions based on their comments. ALT

Despite their broad capabilities, AI tools are more practical to treat as junior colleagues. They are productive, but their suggestions and conclusions need to be checked, and their reasoning needs to be guided in the right direction.
Here are the main problems you may encounter and ways to neutralize them:

rows: Hallucination Many AI tools can invent conclusions even if you feed them specific interview transcripts or open comments.

— Use tools that provide links to specific quotes / numbers from your data so you can quickly check the basis for a conclusion, such as Notebook LM or Breyta — Require specific quotes from the transcript or links to data — Use prompts that define in advance the rules the neural network must follow, including direct instructions such as “do not invent anything”
Misinterpretation In a complex domain, a neural-network model may not understand field-specific nuances and may incorrectly interpret respondents’ words and comments

— Share materials on the topic with the neural network and ask it to interpret answers with the “learned” context in mind — Provide detailed context in the prompt, for example: “Our product offers API integration for hotel inventory”…

Incorrect calculations A request to calculate data using a specific framework, for example Price Sensitivity Meter, may fail — the model may use an invented calculation formula and give false results.
— Choose specialized tools for data analysis, such as specific GPT plugins — Provide the correct calculation formula and ask it to work strictly according to that formula

chapters.title: How to set everything up so research helps the business

blocks.text: Research creates value only when decisions are made based on its results. For this to become normal, two things are needed: trust in the results and a system that reinforces success.
Next, we will look at steps for integrating research. Their order is not universal. Choose what seems most suitable for your situation and context.

children.title: Assess what situation you are in

blocks.text: UX Research Maturity shows how deeply research is embedded in business processes, which methods are suitable at the current stage, and which steps will help you move forward.
There are different maturity models, for example from NN/g or KEIKENDO, which is good for getting started. It shows what place research occupies in a company:

Low maturity: research is conducted occasionally and depends on enthusiasts, or it is not conducted at all. Today, many companies already have research in one form or another. Yes, you will need to invest time in training and show new approaches in practice. But the most important thing is that interest in the topic already exists, and that is an excellent foundation. From there, everything depends on time and leaders’ readiness to move toward greater customer centricity.
Medium maturity: research is embedded in team processes, but its influence on strategy is limited.
High maturity: research systematically manages risks and shapes business decisions.
Model by KEIKENDO ALT
[Russian text from the image]

rows: What it looks like
UX
UX + discourse
UX + discourse + methods
UX + discourse + methods + processes
UX culture + discourse + methods + processes + business
Key barriers
Lack of awareness, rejection
Time, budget, and resource constraints
Formalization, deepening, expansion
Scaling
Tools for advancement
Training
Usability testing
Quantitative methods
Linking UX metrics to KPIs

blocks.text: [details on how to read the image will go here]

rows: UX is not considered a separate practice. It emerges incidentally — as a by-product of business or IT tasks.
The product is designed “for ourselves”: users are idealized, and real data is barely used. UX appears in external communications but remains a slogan.
A dedicated UX specialist or small UX team emerges within the company. Tests are rare and not always methodologically rigorous.
There is a full UX team (information architecture, interaction design, usability). UX techniques are systematic and embedded in the process.
UX has the same organizational standing as marketing and finance. It is embedded in the culture and involved at every stage: discovery → design → development.

blocks.text: Nasdaq’s maturity model is more detailed. It works well for planning the next steps to raise the maturity level.
Usage example: Mark the intersections that are closest to your situation. The result may look like a chessboard. It is rare for a company to fit clearly into one column or row.
Nasdaq UX Research maturity model. ALT
[Russian text from the image]
At the beginning, you do not need to convince everyone with presentations and theory. It is better to show the effect through concrete examples.

children.title: Start with small studies that are visible to the business, and expand the scale step by step

blocks.text: They are easier to run and quickly show results. If after a test users contact support less often, conversion grows, or the task is completed faster, stakeholders themselves start saying “yes” to new research.

Example: Try to find one clear insight that could potentially improve one of the business’s focus metrics. For example, choose an important release and run an unmoderated usability test or a survey to evaluate the design. Then act according to the situation: either show the impact immediately by identifying weak points and redesigning them, or wait for the release and, if the metrics do not meet expectations, explain to the business why that happened and how research helps predict it.

Bonus: And if many point studies have already been run before you, try to look at the bigger picture. Ask yourself: Which key business questions are still unanswered? Which stages of the user journey have we not researched yet or researched only superficially? Are there audience segments we know least about? Which methods have we used, and which could complement them — for example, only surveys without observation, or only qualitative methods without quantitative ones? Which hypotheses have already been confirmed, and which remain untested? How have our past studies affected product decisions, and where was the connection between insights and actions weak or absent? Where are the biggest risks and uncertainties in the product or business right now, and are they covered by research?

Look for people who are ready to support research: from partners-in-crime, people who believe in research as much as you do, to those who at least do not reject it. These may be product managers, marketing, sales, support, development, and other people in the company. The main thing is that together you can demonstrate value in practice.
Example: Launch a pilot study and talk about it at a meetup. One successful case with a clear, vivid insight can turn skeptics first into people who are unsure, and then into supporters.
This lets them see for themselves where users run into difficulties, and they will support improvements faster. The main thing is not to overload them with details.

Examples: Share recordings of unmoderated tests — some people like watching them in full. For key stakeholders, make short highlight reels with moments that show difficulties, successes, thoughts, and emotions of users. This format is more visual and helps insights stick more strongly.
ROI (Return on Investment) is a measure of return on investment in research. It shows how much value, savings, or additional revenue the resources invested in research generated.

Nothing persuades better than numbers and stories confirmed by practice. There are dozens of market examples where UX research directly affected key metrics: increased conversion, reduced support costs, improved retention. Such cases speak to stakeholders in their language — the language of revenue, savings, and growth.

Examples: Use market cases: Virgin America case: after launching a responsive website redesign, the company increased conversion by 14%, reduced support calls by 20%, and users started booking tickets almost twice as fast on all devices. HubSpot case: the company increased revenue by 33%, reaching $1.731 billion compared with the previous year. How? They ran usability tests, tracked satisfaction metrics, and made improvements based on user feedback. Show your own results: calculate the ROI of past studies and share the numbers with key stakeholders.

children.title: Step 2. Embed yourself in business processes

blocks.text: Once trust has been earned, it is important to turn research from one-off work into part of the system.

What matters most at the moment: release speed, release quality, finding product-market fit, maintaining market position, or something else? This will help you understand how to embed research so that it brings value, does not slow the team down, and keeps the right balance between speed, quality, and money.

Examples: Get involved in planning, especially if you work with several teams. At the start, it is worth attending their meetings more often so you become established as part of the team. Get involved in planning. Especially if you work with several teams, at the start it is worth attending their meetings more often so you become established as part of the team and build context. Try to evaluate the project realistically. Especially at the beginning, give teams a positive experience with research. Plan studies independently in advance around project milestones. This shows your foresight. At the beginning of developing a research practice, do not block team work and releases because research is absent. Mandatory usability tests before releases are more appropriate for teams with high UX maturity. For example, in a large bank, this may be justified and necessary. In a small startup trying to reach the market faster and outpace competitors, this approach will only slow growth.
What should you do if you are the first researcher and there are many teams? There is no single correct answer. But there is a plan that can help you orient yourself:
understand the business context and find the balance between speed, quality, and resources
clarify expectations with your manager and key stakeholders
look at the company’s strategic and tactical plans
define what exactly you consider important. This point is key: you are the one who sets the research standard. But form your opinion based on everything listed above

Examples: The company expects strategic influence. Teams already run tactical research and want to continue. It is important to align expectations: focus on strategic projects, and offer teams consulting, methodological support, and help with recruiting. Common sense matters more, and one burned-out researcher will not help anyone. The company wants to improve product quality. Prioritization and the need for features are decided differently. Then it makes sense to focus on tactical projects and processes for different teams: research sign-up, recruiting external and internal users, speed and quality of results, evaluating user experience in production, and so on.

children.title: Build relationships and trust with stakeholders

blocks.text: This is not a bonus, but a working tool that increases the value of research. Relationships provide understanding of business priorities, access to data and users, help align the focus, and make it easier to implement insights in work.

Examples: Regularly share research progress updates with stakeholders. For example, agree that every Monday morning a short status update will appear in the work chat. This reduces uncertainty, saves time on clarification, and shows that you have the process under control. Do not wait until the end of the study to share important findings. If something can influence a business decision right now, report it immediately. The main thing is to separate signal from noise: share only what truly leads to action. Value time — yours and stakeholders’. If an issue can be discussed in 10 minutes instead of half an hour, shorten the meeting. If the key point can be communicated in 15 minutes, do it at the beginning of an hour-long presentation, then let the CPO leave and devote the remaining time to details for those who need them.

children.title: Teach the value of UX research

blocks.text: The more clearly stakeholders see how research helps their goals, the more willingly they support it.

Examples: Run workshops. The NN Group guide describes 5 workshop formats for effective research and team involvement: Discovery workshops. Help capture the current state of the product and agree on plans and key milestones. Empathy workshops. Help the team and stakeholders better understand users and prioritize their needs before design begins. Design workshops. Help generate and discuss many ideas quickly in a diverse group of participants. Prioritization workshops. Help the team agree on which features matter most to users or stakeholders and set priorities. Critique workshops. Check how well design decisions actually meet user needs.

When the team has already seen the value of research, the next step is to make it not one-off, but embedded in product life. This means defining the rules of the game, building a system, and enabling knowledge to accumulate.
A researcher is not a “support service” for the product, but a partner in decision-making. To make that work:

Connect research at the stage of ideas and hypothesis formulation; align with stakeholders before launch: what do we want to achieve, and how will we use the results; create a single knowledge repository, such as YouTrack, Notion, Dovetail, or Confluence — all studies should go there in a convenient format. This saves hours of work, helps avoid repeated tests, and turns scattered findings into a system.
Fix a report template: 1–2 slides with the task, method, findings, and product decision. Then any team member can quickly skim the most important parts of the results.
Organize cross-functional interaction. A good practice is to make research not only for researchers:

Create a shared research calendar so everyone knows what is already in progress and where they can join; invite product managers, designers, and marketers to observe interviews or tests, so insights are perceived firsthand; after each study, formulate action items: what exactly the team should try, change, or test; build a feedback loop — come back in a month and check whether the metric or user behavior changed after the insights were implemented. This will reinforce the value of research: it will stop being a “collection of interesting facts” and become a working tool that influences the product.

Research democratization is useful only where there are clear boundaries. Giving people a tool does not mean they know how to use it, and a one-day workshop does not turn a stakeholder into a researcher. If the system is not built, instead of value you get “research theater”: insights stop being taken seriously, and research becomes imitation.

What works in practice: Define which studies can be delegated and which cannot. It is safe to delegate simple usability tests, short surveys, or hallway tests. But strategic discovery, segmentation, or in-depth interviews remain in researchers’ area. Create a “living document” listing tasks that can and cannot be delegated. Define access levels. For example: Observer (watches interviews), Assistant (runs simple tests using a template), Lead (can assemble a project with mentorship). Each level should have clear expectations and boundaries. Build training as a system, not a one-off workshop. Design stages: “basics” → “intermediate level” → “partnership.” Pair every newcomer with a mentor and give them shadow sessions with feedback. Focus on outcomes, not activity. Count not “how many people tried to run a test,” but “which decisions were made based on their results.” This helps you see value and adjust the system in time. Use democratization metrics for hiring. If suddenly more and more research goes to non-researchers, it is not a signal to “reduce the load,” but a signal that it is time to hire a researcher.
If democratization replaces hiring researchers, the system is built incorrectly.
